topic: A homeowner learns their house was a former crime scene
raw_output:
[story 1]

The realtor’s cheerful demeanor grated on Elias’s nerves like sandpaper on raw wood. He kept running a hand over the dusty baseboard, the cheap veneer peeling away to reveal a darker, older layer beneath. Every corner of the living room seemed to hold a shadow, a lingering impression of something violent that the fresh paint couldn't entirely scrub away. He remembered the news reports, the hushed tones about the unsolved incident that had occurred in this very structure decades ago. A tight knot formed in his stomach, constricting his chest. He didn't want to feel anything but the sterile calm of a fresh start, but the air in the hallway felt thick, heavy with unspoken history. He pressed his forehead against the cool plaster, a low, involuntary groan escaping him. This house wasn't a sanctuary; it was a mausoleum, and he was forced to live in the crypt.

[story 2]

“It’s charming, really,” the agent chirped, gesturing toward a darkened study. Sarah didn't look up from the floorboards. Her jaw was clenched so tightly a muscle jumped beneath her ear. She traced a faint, almost invisible smear on the edge of the hardwood near the fireplace—a smudge she couldn't quite place. It was the kind of residue that defied cleaning, the ghost of something awful. She stood up abruptly, the sound echoing too loudly in the silent rooms. Her gaze swept over the antique furniture, cataloging every scratch, every misplaced item, as if searching for the precise moment the tragedy had unfolded. A sharp, involuntary shudder ran through her, and she quickly smoothed her expression into a mask of polite indifference, though her knuckles were white where she gripped the edge of the doorway.

[story 3]

I walked through the foyer, and the silence hit me like a physical blow. It wasn't peaceful; it was expectant. I could feel the weight of every unseen event pressing down on the floorboards beneath my expensive shoes. The wallpaper in the upstairs hallway seemed to watch me, its faded pattern mocking my attempts to impose order. I stopped in the kitchen, staring at the pantry door. It wasn't the clutter that bothered me—I could manage clutter—it was the implication. The way the light fell across the linoleum seemed to highlight faint, almost imperceptible discolorations near the back wall. A slow, simmering resentment began to bubble up, hot and corrosive in my throat. I turned away, the motion stiff, and walked toward the front door, needing to escape the suffocating atmosphere of the place.
